title: India Locks Bangladesh Into Regional Energy Grid—and Reshapes South Asian Infrastructure
lede: By binding Bangladesh to Indian power supplies and transport corridors through binding infrastructure agreements, New Delhi has secured its most strategically vital neighbor within an asymmetric economic architecture that will shape subregional politics for a generation.

sections.context: Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina's September 2022 state visit to India produced the region's most comprehensive connectivity framework since the 1972 Friendship Treaty. The centerpiece—a multi-modal infrastructure MOU spanning rail freight, inland waterway protocols, and power trade—represents New Delhi's deliberate strategy to embed Bangladesh within Indian logistics and energy systems at a moment when Dhaka faces acute electricity deficits and deteriorating fiscal space. The immediate driver is functional: Bangladesh requires 1,160 MW of Indian power imports to prevent rolling blackouts during its monsoon-constrained hydroelectric season, a dependency that New Delhi is now formalizing through long-term supply contracts rather than ad-hoc arrangements. But the deeper significance lies in trajectory. By simultaneously advancing three separate connectivity pathways—the Maitree Super Thermal Power Plant at Rampal (co-developed with Bangladesh Power Development Board), inland waterway protocols that will reduce transport costs for Indian goods flowing into Bangladesh, and rail freight corridors that bypass Pakistan entirely—India is constructing a physical infrastructure that makes Bangladesh economically subordinate to Indian supply chains rather than diversifying them. This is not coercion; it is structural integration. Hasina's government, politically aligned with New Delhi and hostile to Pakistan-leaning opposition factions, has willingly accepted this asymmetry. Yet the architecture created in September 2022 contains within it the seeds of future friction: power dependency creates leverage; infrastructure integration creates expectations of preferential access to Indian markets that India may not grant; and the explicit exclusion of alternative corridors (through Pakistan, via China) leaves Bangladesh with diminishing optionality.
sections.threads: This MOU belongs to a longer pattern of India using infrastructure as a tool of strategic adjacency with smaller neighbors—a doctrine that emerged clearly after the 2008 [[india-bangladesh-water-sharing-2009|Ganges Water Sharing Treaty]], which established the principle that bilateral infrastructure agreements could restructure hydro-political relationships. The September 2022 connectivity framework extends that logic from water to electricity to transport. Simultaneously, it reflects India's broader strategic pivot toward what New Delhi calls 'Act East'—the deliberate deepening of ties with Southeast Asian neighbors (Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand) to counterbalance Chinese Belt and Road influence in the region. The Maitrie power plant itself represents a partial hedge: it is not Chinese-financed infrastructure (as much Bangladeshi development is), but Indian-partnered, which signals to Dhaka that energy security need not mean Beijing-dependence. Yet the connectivity MOU also intersects with India's internal northeast development agenda. Rail and waterway corridors that move Indian freight through Bangladesh ultimately serve Indian manufacturers in Assam and Meghalaya seeking cheaper access to Bay of Bengal ports. The infrastructure benefits Bangladesh in reduced transport costs, but the commercial logic flows from Delhi, not Dhaka. This mirrors the logic of China's Belt and Road projects in South Asia—infrastructure that claims reciprocal benefit but structures underlying power asymmetrically. The critical difference is that Hasina's government has chosen Indian partnership over Chinese, a choice made plausible by shared strategic antagonism toward Pakistan and by Hasina's own political alignment with the Indian establishment.
sections.signal: Analysts should track three specific indicators over the next 18 months. First, the operationalization timeline for the Maitrie power plant at Rampal: any delays beyond the projected 2024 completion would signal either technical friction between NTPC and Bangladesh Power Development Board or changing political will in Dhaka. Second, the volume of actual power flows: the agreed 1,160 MW is a ceiling, not a floor. Monitor whether Bangladesh actually imports at or near this volume or whether domestic generation (including coal and renewable sources) stabilizes demand below contracted volumes—a signal of whether energy integration is deepening or stalling. Third, the most sensitive metric: the political composition of Bangladesh's next government. Hasina's Awami League has engineered this infrastructure tilt toward India, but Bangladesh's opposition Bangladesh Nationalist Party (BNP) has historically maintained closer ties to Pakistan and expressed skepticism of asymmetric Indian integration. Elections are scheduled for 2024. A BNP-led government would likely renegotiate the terms of infrastructure dependency, potentially accelerating Chinese engagement in rail and power sectors as an alternative. Watch for any statements from opposition parties criticizing the MOU as surrendering energy sovereignty. Fourth, implementation of the inland waterway protocol: successful activation of the Brahmaputra-Ganges corridor would represent the most concrete measure of infrastructure deepening. Delays or disputes over cargo rights would signal resistance to the full logic of the integration framework.
